speaker: interviewer
text: --with Adela Manheimer. It's April 9, 1995. Go ahead, Adela.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not-- I know that. But I have to say go ahead, I can't just end it up. Adela, tell us more what happened after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I-- I was raising my son, and after five years later-- five and a half-- my daughter was born in Cleveland, Ohio, and my son helped me a lot to raise my daughter. They were very close until now. They never had an argument between themselves, a brother and a sister, because they were helping their mother and father. And I was the bread giver. I was working all the years, and my husband was working, maybe all together, maybe a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a mechanic-- a precision mechanic-- and he was working for the Lear Corporation here in Los Angeles. But then-- then got slow, and he was the last one to be hired, so they let him go, and it was very hard on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you, Adela, what was your profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- my profession? I couldn't get to a profession, so I was taking care for a-- in a convalescent home in the office for a little time, and then I was managing a-- a wholesale clothing shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Cleveland, Ohio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cleveland I was working in a factory of a-- packing locks, all kind of-- I was working machines, and very hard. I was working in back alleys, whatever it took to make a living for my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to come to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and my husband's family was living in Los Angeles. They came from Belgium-- Brussels. And the weather in Cleveland was impossible, so we decided to come here. And-- and we stay here. It was much better than Ohio, and I raised here the-- the children, and I worked, and I never really was sad for myself, because I was very appreciative being alive, after what I went through. I always think, it could be worse. I couldn't have a family, now I have a family, and I thank God for this. Every time was very down to earth, humble, very. Till today, I appreciate every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your husband die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband died October 10th, '84.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 Years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I don't know-- somehow I was 47 times, 37 times back and forth, UCLA, and here, and here. And I was always from 8:00 till 9:00 behind the bed, but I still sometimes regret that maybe I did something wrong, and this is a very bad thing, what I live with.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think you should feel this way, because knowing about the Holocaust, and knowing about survival--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and knowing what they went through like you know and I know, Adela.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. It was-- this is the feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was wounded, he was working underground, and he was very sick. And what you did is give him more life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave him support. I gave him physical, and mentally, and material support. Everything could be done, but I still think-- because the last day of his life, I didn't go. One day, I didn't go to the convalescent home-- whatever he was, temporarily-- and this is the day where he passed away. And that's why it's my conscience.

speaker: interviewer
text: It shouldn't be. I want to make you feel good, because you know, you had an obligation to your children, too. So don't feel badly about it, because like you said, for how many years for you to get there afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from '45-- the end of '45-- until '84.

speaker: interviewer
text: Practically 40 years. Practically 40 years, you've taken care of your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Last week on the 30th of March was 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were married. And you told me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about your son, that he has a very good education.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now I'm talking about my son. OK. My son pushed himself through all his education. He went to UCLA, and alone, he learned himself how to swim, how to-- how to ride a bike, everything himself. And he was always unbelievable good and calm. No-- and no complaints. I always said, other-- other boys go with their father to baseball, to this, you never have a chance. He says, it's OK. Don't worry about it. You are great, and Papa is great. Everything was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how-- the children-- my statement is, the children what was raised in not such a-- a big luxury, they came out very, very good, and the best professionals and the best people. The children that are raised with all the luxury, reaching for dope. And then it comes one day, they don't have what they needed, they commit suicide, and nothing is good. So this-- when I say something my children say, what do you want? You have great two kids, and you don't owe us nothing. You were always good, we had everything we needed. That's how they-- I think that's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're very proud of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have how many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three boys and three girls, and they're all good-- very good. Good in school, and good behavior. Everything's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adela, can you tell me something else? As long as your husband is not here to give testimony, he must have told you how it happened to him when he ran away. Could you tell us anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On the train where they took him-- I don't know where. Just like a march, like we do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except they took him by cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, open thing. So he looked at other people, and they cut the veins, they cut themselves. They got crazy from hunger, from everything. So he said to himself, what I can lose when I run-- when I run--

speaker: interviewer
text: And which camp did they take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a running train. He jumped off, and the guy what saw him, it shoot after him, and he shot him in the back. The [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The German, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So he saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me where? Which camp was that near? Was it near Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably near Mauthausen, yes. When this-- when this happened, he saw a little house, and he knocked the door. And they got very scared, and they decide to be very friendly, and they told him, come in. And when he come in, they-- they went in the other room and they called the police, the Germans. And when they come in, they took him to Mauthausen, to the mountains. And there was a gypsy kapos, and they beat him all the time. And he had all blue here on his hand from the beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told you all this, didn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he tell me everything all the time. But he never complained. He said, you cannot walk backwards, and you have to make the best-- this was his character-- the best from the day. And even when he was very, very sick-- he was nine years on a-- on a machine, a respirator, what he couldn't live by himself. Everything, the machine lived for him. And I operated this machine, which a lot of nurses and doctors doesn't even know. It's very precision, with little, little, little thing. He never complained, and the doctors couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctors said, how he doesn't complain? Every time I come in to ask, how are you, Wolf, He said, fine, good. They said such a patient they never had. They stick him every day with gases, which is very painful, and has to be on a-- on a narcos. Narcotic.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he just rigid like nothing. He has to do, has to do, and he never complained. And nobody can believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a double survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Double survivor. He survived 40 years being so sick, and-- and probably five years or four years in the Auschwitz concentration.

speaker: interviewer
text: The camps. He was in Auschwitz, and then in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Goes in Auschwitz, Mauthausen-- all good places. OK. So I want to go now to my-- yeah. Mt son pushed himself with his education very hard, and he said he cannot be a doctor, he cannot look at blood. He cannot be a this, he cannot be this. He likes to be a writer, so he became an editor. And he is in the-- in the Hebrew Union College. He became-- go through this school, and he became in New York-- he lives in New York-- and he became an editor. He's very popular, a good reputation, and a lot [INAUDIBLE]. He's going to write up the whole Czechoslovakia trip-- the Jewish life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the Czechoslovakia trip. Because when I interviewed-- the pre-interview I did with you, you told me you are leaving for Czechoslovakia to meet a liberator of yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, but I like to finish with my daughter and then go to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And then in '53-- in '53, my daughter was born, and then-- and my son took care of her, because I had to go to work, and their father never felt too good. But anyway, this is the story that she also push herself. And she became a body improvement exercise teacher, which she's teaching in Beverly Hills school for many years. She's very loved, and I'm her student, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: It looks good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she said that this is not a profession forever, when you get a little older. She got into photography. We give you this-- and she is now going for extra courses to UCLA, behind those machine-- expensive machine-- and she's a very good mother, has three beautiful children, and-- and a good marriage. What else? And my son has a good marriage. He married a girl from Vancouver, British Columbia, and-- and the father is a psychiatrist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they have any children, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have three children, yeah. Very good. So when they-- when I heard from the mayor from Prague, a letter which I have, that the ghettos what was liberated in his city, he would like to make a ceremony for three days to take from Prague to Volary and to meet them all. My children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Volary is that little town that you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So my children decided to leave everything, the kids, the home-- we're never together. They are busy. My son lives in Connecticut and works in New York. Five hours every day to travel. So we decided-- they decide to go with me. I want my husband to go. He didn't-- he couldn't go, because he'd been in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it would be hard on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, so who is that liberator that you were going to meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, it was American soldier which liberated us, and he met one of our girls, which was from Greenberg, and they got married. And somehow he decided to be close to this subject, and he went to Greenberg. He went to Volary--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Greenburg? Greenberg is a town in--

speaker: interviewee
text: By Breslau, belongs now to Poland. And he decided to give his life for this-- for this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Purpose.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so-- and his name is Bernard Robertson. He lives in Westwood, and he went few times to Czechoslovakia. And now he's organizing this trip. And with him, and another girl from Israel organizing, they-- we all come together. It's a big expense, but I don't mind. I never go no place. I wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adela, did you ever meet Tziporah again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Tziporah. I went to Israel and I met her. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you right now, I lost her track. I don't even know if she's alive, and I'm very ashamed of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It can happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm planning-- being-- because she-- she had a-- she married, and her husband passed away. She lives in one of these Arabic-- in Haifa-- Arabic homes, which is a lot of stairs up and down. And probably the age-- she was older, and I don't know what happened. I should know. I am very guilty.

speaker: interviewer
text: See, you shouldn't feel badly, because you had a hard life with your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah Yeah, I want to tell you now the things about-- yeah, I want to finish with my son. My son became very popular, and he is working for the Holocaust. I give you-- I show you all the papers. He's giving speeches, and he's teaching in Connecticut in the biggest temple in Westport. He's teaching teenagers about Holocaust. He's teaching on Sundays, and I'm proud of this. We're always in contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he get-- did he get a degree in-- in teaching on the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I don't know if he had a degree, but I know he's teaching, and he's involved in-- in everything. So when I-- when you called me about this appointment, somebody connect me to a girl which is-- she's an international communicator manager, and her name is Susan Cochran. She's going to Russia and she's going to Prague, so I'd like her to meet my son there, because they could write up and do something with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because he-- he has a magazine, and-- and I will show you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean he writes in a magazine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, every month. I have a magazine. He's the editor. I'll show you. There is a-- later, yeah. OK, this is finished. My daughter's finished. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that's a marvelous thing, that a second generation is-- from a survivor. And your son must feel that he owes it to his father and to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he writes about his father. He's every time mention me, and he doesn't have time now to write a book about me, but he promised. Because he has to make a living and has to-- His children go to private college, and everything has to be done. OK, I like to mention about my present husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were friends. His wife and him, we were friends. And we belong to one society which is a landsman society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is him? You better tell his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Solomon Urman. And Blanche.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Blanche.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always together, because it was a society for landsman that's from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bedzin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bedzin. And [SIGHS] when this happened, it's almost in one year. His wife-- Solomon's wife passed away and my husband passed away. And I don't know how, we just met in Fairfax. And he left to Israel, he came back. And he came to my house, and we start, like, dating. And when I saw he bought me a little watch from the airport I said, something is not really normal. Something is more than that. But after-- after two years dating-- actually three years, we're dating-- I moved in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You moved in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I moved in here, and we are together, like, eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight years.

speaker: interviewee
text: In November was eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're-- this is confidential, everything on this tape is confidential. You are married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are married just with the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I see it's a very happy relationship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because Solomon is a beautiful person, and you're a beautiful person, Adela.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we are-- we are very sincere, honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now what I'm going to do is I'm going to let you and Solomon show some pictures from your family, and I'm going to get out of this chair and let you take over with our photographer, Allison.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finish with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much. Is there anything-- is there anything that you forgot that you wanted to tell me? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, let me think.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have-- I have all day for you. Just tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, beside all the papers--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't want you-- I want you to tell it from your heart and what you went through. Not papers that been written down. Please, can you remember something else that happened to you? During the march or during the thing-- when you found out--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the march-- during the march, the whole march, I can tell you, in my dreams, in my daily life, that I don't believe myself that I went through. Because I think this is just-- just an episode, a dream, a legend. What can you name-- that when people are here in a normal, beautiful, life, catch colds, viruses, and all kind of sickness, and your walk five months with no food in the snow. You eat snow dirty with dust, with everything, and you don't have a virus, and you don't have a cold. Just a little boil from dirt, that's all. So I believe in miracles, and I believe that everybody what is alive should be good to each other, not look for problems, look for good, and have really a positive thinking. But that helps for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the legacy your father left you, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He left me this. That you just can take your name, but you cannot take money, not even a check, nothing. Nothing. Not jewelry, everything is-- is-- is nothing. It's just your name. And I live by that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that means that you are supposed to be a nice person, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I try. I try for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me tell you something, you are, Adela. You're a very nice person, and I'm-- I'm richer by knowing you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I'm richer by knowing you. Such a celebrity.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not. I'm not. I'm just a--

speaker: interviewee
text: When you tell me you run to Sweden to interview--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no darling. I'm just a survivor like you and interviewing for the Shoah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I hope in the near future we meet, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sure we will. Thank you.
